title: Lathyrus incurvus
taxonomy: Lathyrus incurvus is classified as a species of flowering plant in the pea family Fabaceae. It is a perennial legume native to the Mediterranean region. Lathyrus incurvus is further classified within the genus Lathyrus, which is a group of vetches and sweet peas. The exact classification within the genus is Lathyrus incurvus (L.) L. However, it's worth noting that classifications can sometimes vary across different sources.

side_effects: Lathyrus incurvus is known to contain a toxin called ODAP (oxalyldiaminopropionic acid), which can cause neurolathyrism, a condition that affects the nervous system. This condition can lead to muscle paralysis, tremors, and in severe cases, death. Consuming large quantities of the plant's seeds can result in the development of this condition. The toxin is most potent when consumed in large amounts, making it essential to avoid overconsumption. Neurolathyrism is a rare condition, but it can be prevented by limiting or avoiding consumption of the plant altogether.
intro: Lathyrus incurvus, also known as the hairy vetchling, is a species of flowering plant in the pea family (Fabaceae). The plant has been found to possess various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which have been traditionally used to treat respiratory issues and promote overall well-being. Bioactive constituents of Lathyrus incurvus include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, Lathyrus incurvus is often used to make herbal teas, infusions, and decoctions, and is sometimes used in combination with other herbs to treat colds, coughs, and other respiratory ailments.
